When πj(a | s) = 0 at any state s, KL divergence is +∞. Since the trajectories of these policies have disjoint states, DKL A (π1, π2) = DKL A (π1, π3) = + ∞. Similar results can be obtained for cross-entropy. JSDγ JSDγ was defined in [38] and we consider two special cases when γ = 0 and γ = 1. As illustrated by [ 38], JSD0 measures the expected number of times two policies will “disagree” by selecting different actions. On trajectories induced by π1 and π2, there are 4 + 4 states that π1 disagrees with π2 (π1 and π2 are symmetric) and DJSD0 A (π1, π2) = 8/16 = 1/2. Similarly, π1 and π3 only disagree at the initial state, therefore we have DJSD0 A (π1, π3) = 2/16 = 1/8. JSD1 is defined by JSD1(πi, πj) = − 1 2 X τi P(τi | πi) TX t=1 1 T log πi(τi) + πj(τi) 2πi(τi) − 1 2 X τj P(τj | πj) TX t=1 1 T log πi(τj) + πj(τj) 2πj(τj) . Since each of the policies considered only induces a single trajectory and πi(τj) = 0 (i ̸= j), we can easily compute DJSD1 A (π1, π2) = DJSD1 A (π1, π3) = log 2 Wasserstein Distance Wasserstein distance or Earth Moving Distance (EMD) is 1 if two policies disagree on a state and 0 otherwise. Therefore, it equals to DJSD0 A . B.6.2 Action Norm We embed the action “right” as vector[1, 0] since it increases the x-coordinate by 1 and the action “down” as vector [0, −1] since it decreases the y-coordinate by 1. This embedding can be naturally extended to a continuous action space with velocity actions. Following [ 52], we compute the action norm over a uniform distribution on states. We can see that there are7 states where π1 and π2 perform differently and 1 state (the initial state) where π1 and π3 perform differently. Therefore, we can get D(π1, π2) = √ 7 and D(π1, π3) = 1. B.6.3 State-Distance-Based Measures State L2 Norm Similar to action L2 norm, we concatenate the coordinates instead of actions as the embedding and compute the L2 norm between embedding. Figure 12: Policies in the grid-world example when NG = 5. 22Wasserstein Distance Wasserstein distance is tractable in the grid-world example. We consider 7 states (except the initial and final states) in each trajectory and compute the pair-wise distance as matrix C. Then we solve the following linear programming min γ X i,j γ ⊙ C s.t. γ1 = a, γT 1 = b γi,j ≥ 0 where ⊙ means element-wise multiplication, 1 is a all-one vector, a = [1T , 0T ]T and b = [0T , 1T ]T is the marginal state distribution of each policy. C Environment Details C.1 Details of the 2D Navigation Environment The navigation environment has an agent circle with size a and 4 landmark circles with size b. We pre-specify a threshold c and constrain that the distance of final states reaching different landmarks must be larger than c. Correspondingly, landmark circles are randomly initialized by constraining the pairwise distance between centers to be larger than a threshold c + 2(a + b) such that the final-state constraint is valid. An episode ends if the agent touches any landmarks, i.e., the distance between the center of the agent and the center of the landmark d < a+ b, or 1000 timesteps have elapsed. The observation space includes the positions of the agent and all landmarks, which is a 10-dimensional vector. The action space is a 2-dimensional vector, which is the agent velocity. The time interval is set to be ∆t = 0.1, i.e., the next position is computed by xt+1 = xt + ∆t · v. The reward is 1 if the agent touches the landmark and 0 otherwise. C.2 Details of Environments We provide training configurations and environment introductions below and refer readers to our project website in App. A for visualizations of these environments. Humanoid We use the Humanoid environment in IsaacGym [ 42] with default observation and action spaces. The input of intrinsic rewards or diversity measure is the observation without all torque states. SMAC We adopt the SMAC environment in the MAPPO codebase2 with the same configuration as Yu et al. [69]. The input of intrinsic rewards or diversity measure is the state of all allies, including positions, health, etc. On the “easy” map 2m_vs_1z, two marines must be controlled to defeat a Zealot. The marines can attack from a distance, while the Zealot’s attacks are limited to close range. A successful strategy involves alternating the marines’ attacks to distract the Zealot. On the “hard” map 2c_vs_64zg, two colossi must be controlled by the agents to fight against 64 zergs. The colossi have a wider attack range and can move over cliffs. Strategies on this map may include hit-and-run tactics, waiting in corners, or dividing and conquering enemies. The level of difficulty is determined by the learning performance of existing MARL algorithms. Harder maps require more exploration and training steps. GRF We adopt the “simple115v2” representation as observation with both “scoring” and “check- point” reward. The reward is shared across all agents. The input of intrinsic rewards or diversity measure is the position and velocity of all attackers and the ball. All policies are trained to control the left team to score against built-in bots. academy_3_vs_1_with_keeper: In this scenario, a team of three players (left) tries to score a goal against a single defender and a goalkeeper. The left team starts with the ball and has to dribble past the defender and the goalkeeper to score a goal. 2https://github.com/marlbenchmark/on-policy 23Table 15: Hyperparameters in the 2D navigation environment. discount GAE λ PPO epochs clip parameter entropy bonus λmax actor lr critic lr Lagrange lr batch size 0.997 0.95 10 0.2 0 10 3e-4 1e-3 0.5 4000 Table 16: Common hyperparameters for SIPO, baselines, and ablations. discount GAE λ actor lr critic lr clip parameter entropy bonus GRF batch size SMAC batch size 0.99 0.95 5e-4 1e-3 0.2 0.01 9600 3200 academy_counterattack_easy: In this scenario, the left team starts with the ball in the front yard and tries to score a goal against several defenders. All eleven players in the left players can be controlled. academy_corner: In this scenario, the left team tries to score a goal from a corner kick. The right team defends the goal and tries to prevent the left team from scoring. All eleven players in the left players can be controlled. D Implementation Details D.1 2D Navigation We apply PPO with Lagrange multipliers to optimize the policy and hyperparameters are summarized in Table 15. D(πi, πj) is simply taken as the L2 distance of the final state reached by πi and πj, i.e., D(πi, πj) = ∥sπi H − sπj H ∥2. The applied algorithm is the same as SIPO (see Appendix G) except that the intrinsic reward is only computed at the last timestep. D.2 SIPO In the i-th iteration ( 1 ≤ i ≤ M), we learn an actor and a critic with i separate value heads to accurately predict different return terms, including i − 1 intrinsic returns for the diversity constraints and the environment reward. We include all practical tricks mentioned in [ 69] because we find them all critical to algorithm performance. We use separate actor and critic networks, both with hidden size 64 and a GRU layer with hidden size 64. The common hyperparameters for SIPO, baselines, and ablations are listed in Table 16. Other environment-specific parameters, such as PPO epochs and mini-batch size, are all the same as [69]. Besides, Table 14 and Table 17 lists some extra hyperparameters for SIPO. D.3 Baselines We re-implement all baselines with PPO based on the MAPPO [69] project. All algorithms run for the same number of environment frames. Specific hyperparameters for baselines can be found in Appendix D.3. SMERL SMERL trains a latent-conditioned policy that can robustly adapt to new scenarios. It promotes diversity by maximizing the mutual information between states and the latent variable. We implement SMERL with PPO, where the actor and the critic take as the input the concatenation of observation and a one-hot latent variable. The discriminator is a 2-layer feed-forward network with 64 hidden units. The learning rate of the discriminator is the same as the learning rate of the critic network. The input of the discriminator is the same as the input we use for SIPO-WD. The critic has 2 value heads for an accurate estimation of intrinsic return. Since SMERL trains a Table 17: SIPO hyperparameters across all environments. λmax Discriminator lr Lagrangian lr 10 4.0e-4 0.1 24single latent-conditioned policy, we train SMERL for M× more environment steps, such that total environment frames are the same. The scaling factor of intrinsic rewards is 0.1 and the threshold for diversification is [0.81, 0.45, 0.72] (0.9 × [0.9, 0.5, 0.8]) for “3v1”, “counterattack”, and “corner” respectively. DvD DvD simultaneously trains a population of policies to maximize the determinant of a kernel matrix based on action difference. We concatenate the one-hot actions along a trajectory as the behavioral embedding. The square of the variance factor, i.e., σ2 in the RBF kernel, is set to be the length of behavioral embedding. We also use the same Bayesian bandits as proposed in the original paper. Training DvD in “counterattack” and “corner” exceeds the GPU memory and we exclude the results in the main body. DIPG DIPG iteratively maximizes the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) distance between the state distribution of the current policy and previously discovered policies. For DIPG, we follow the open-source implementation3. We set the same variance factor in the RBF kernel as SIPO-RBF and apply the same state as the input of the RBF kernel. We sweep the coefficient of MMD loss among {0.1, 0.5, 0.9} and find 0.1 the most appropriate (larger value will cause training instability). We use the same method to save archived trajectories as SIPO and the input of the RBF kernel is the same as the input we use for SIPO-RBF. To improve training efficiency, we only back-propagate the MMD loss at the first PPO epoch. RSPO RSPO iteratively discovers diverse policies by optimizing extrinsic rewards on novel tra- jectories while optimizing diversity on other trajectories. The diversity measure is defined as the action-cross entropy along the trajectory. For RSPO, we follow the opensource implementation 4 and use the same hyperparameters on the SMAC 2c_vs_64zg map in the original paper for GRF experiments. TrajDi TrajDi was originally designed for cooperative multi-agent domains to facilitate zero- shot coordination. It defines a generalized Jensen-Shanon divergence objective between policy action distributions. Then this objective and rewards are simultaneously optimized via population- based training. We tried TrajDi in SMAC and GRF. We sweep the action discount factor among {0.1, 0.5, 0.9} and the coefficient of TrajDi loss among {0.1, 0.01, 0.001}. However, TrajDi fails to converge in the “3v1” scenario and exceeds the GPU memory in the “counterattack” and “corner” scenarios. Therefore, we exclude the performance of TrajDi in the main body. Domino We have meticulously re-implemented Domino within our codebase according to the appendix of Zahavy et al.[71]. We execute the algorithm in the Humanoid locomotion task, employing the robot state (excluding torques) for successor feature computation. Despite our earnest efforts to optimize Domino’s performance, our findings reveal its comparable performance to SMERL, illustrated by a minimal diversity score of 0.01. Therefore, we exclude the performance of Domino in the main body. APT APT maximized the nearest neighbor state-entropy estimation for skill discovery. While we also adopted this metric for diversity evaluation, there is a fundamental distinction in formulation. APT optimizes state entropy within a single policy, whereas our method, SIPO, targets the joint entropy of a population of policies. It is okay for each single policy within the population to have low state entropy. To employ APT’s objective of discovering diverse policies, training a population of agents concurrently is required. The algorithm should optimize the estimated entropy over states visited by all policies. Yet, this approach mandates large-scale k-NN computation (k=12) over substantial batches, leading to significant computational inefficiency. Despite our dedicated efforts, we didn’t finish a single training trial of APT within 48 hours (in contrast to other PBT baselines, e.g. DvD, which completes training in less than 8 hours). D.4 Ablation Study Details For the three ablation studies: fix-L, CE, and filter, we list the specific hyperparameters here: 3https://github.com/dtak/DIPG-public 4https://github.com/footoredo/rspo-iclr-2022 25• fix-L: we set the Lagrange multiplier to be 0.2; • CE: the threshold is 3.800 and the intrinsic reward scale factor is 1/1000 of that in the WD setting; • filter: all the hyperparameters in the setting are the same as those in the WD setting. F Discussion F.1 The Failure Case of State-Distance-Based Diversity Measures A failure case of state-distance-based diversity measures may be when the state space includes many irrelevant features. These features cannot reflect behavioral differences. If we run SIPO in such an environment, the learned strategies may be only diverse w.r.t these features and have little visual distinction. Like the famous noisy TV problem [ 5], the issue of irrelevant features is intrinsically challenging for general RL applications, which cannot be resolved by using action-based or state-occupancy-based diversity measures either. Thanks to the advantages we discussed in the paper, we generally find that state-distance-based measures can be preferred in challenging RL problems. Meanwhile, since the state dimension can be much higher than actions, it is possible that RL optimization over states may be accordingly more difficult than actions. In practice, we can design a feature selector for those most relevant features for visual diversity and run diversity learning over the filtered features. In SMAC and GRF, we utilize the agent features (excluding enemies) as the input of diversity constraint without further modifications, as discussed in Appendix D. We remark that even after filtering, the agent features remain high-dimensional while our algorithm still works well. Note that using a feature selector is a common practice in many existing domains, such as novelty search [ 12], exploration [33], and curriculum learning [7]. There are also works studying how to extract useful low-dimensional features from observations [68, 18], which are orthogonal to our focus. 28F.2 The Distance Metric In Sec. 5, we adopt the two most popular implementations in the machine learning literature, i.e., RBF kernel and Wasserstein distance, while it is totally fine to adopt alternative implementations. For example, we can learn state representations (e.g. auto-encoder, Laplacian, or successor feature) and utilize pair-wise distance or norms as a diversity measure. Similar topics have been extensively discussed in the exploration literature [68, 41]. We leave them as our future directions. G Pseudocode of SIPO The pseudocode of SIPO is shown in Algorithm 1. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: The paper addresses the challenge of discovering diverse, high-reward policies in complex reinforcement learning problems. Its main contributions are: 1) Identifying the failure cases of existing action-based and state-occupancy-based diversity measures and proposing a new measure that explicitly incorporates state-space distance. 2) A theoretical and empirical comparison of Population-Based Training (PBT) and Iterative Learning (ITR) frameworks, showing that ITR can achieve comparable quality to PBT with higher computational efficiency. 3) The development of a novel algorithm, State-based Intrinsic-reward Policy Optimization (SIPO), which combines the ITR framework with the proposed state-distance measure and has provable convergence properties.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: The proposed method, SIPO, uses an Iterative Learning (ITR) framework to discover a set of diverse policies sequentially. In each iteration, it solves a constrained optimization problem to maximize the task reward while ensuring the new policy is sufficiently distant from previously discovered ones. This constrained problem is converted into an unconstrained minimax problem using the Lagrange method and solved via two-timescale Gradient Descent Ascent (GDA). The state-distance diversity measure is formulated as an intrinsic reward, allowing for optimization via policy gradient methods. Two practical implementations of this measure are provided: one using a Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernel (SIPO-RBF) and another using Wasserstein distance with a learnable Lipschitz function (SIPO-WD).
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: The experiments were conducted across three domains: single-agent Humanoid locomotion in Isaac Gym, and multi-agent games StarCraft Multi-Agent Challenge (SMAC) and Google Research Football (GRF). The performance of SIPO was compared against several baselines, including DIPG, SMERL, DvD, and RSPO. Diversity was evaluated using domain-specific metrics: pairwise distance of joint torques for Humanoid, k-nearest-neighbor state entropy for SMAC, and the number of distinct ball-passing routes (a custom metric) for GRF, as standard metrics were found to be misleading in that environment. Task performance (e.g., win rates, episode returns) was also measured.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: The paper identifies two main limitations. First, the method assumes direct access to an object-centric state representation, which may not be available in scenarios with high-dimensional inputs like images, where representation learning would be a necessary prerequisite. Second, the Iterative Learning (ITR) framework is inherently sequential, which can lead to longer wall-clock training times compared to parallelized Population-Based Training (PBT) approaches when the total number of training samples is fixed.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: The authors suggest several directions for future research. These include: 1) Addressing the limitations by incorporating representation learning for high-dimensional observations and exploring methods to accelerate the sequential ITR framework. 2) Developing a more formal, theoretical understanding of what constitutes distinct solutions in RL. 3) Extending the SIPO method to multi-agent competitive games, beyond the cooperative settings tested. 4) Investigating methods to automatically determine the optimal population size to balance computational resources with the diversity of the discovered policies.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_code:
The SIPO method is implemented within the PPO algorithm update rule. The core logic for calculating the diversity measure is in `rspo/multi_agent/agent.py`, where the likelihood of the current policy's actions is evaluated under previously discovered reference policies. This likelihood serves as the state-distance diversity measure.

```python
    # ...
    valid_rollouts.rewards[step: step + episode_steps, :, 1 + rnd_size: sample_n_ref + 1] = reward_prediction_reward
    valid_rollouts.rewards[step: step + episode_steps, :, sample_n_ref + 1 + rnd_size: sample_n_ref * 2 + 1] = ts(normalized_t_dis)
    valid_rollouts.rewards[step: step + episode_steps, :, sample_n_ref * 2 + 1 + rnd_size:] = ts(t_dis)
```
The constrained optimization is handled by shaping the rewards. The `RolloutStorage.compute_interpolate_masks` method in `rspo/storage.py` creates masks to combine the task reward and the diversity-based intrinsic reward, effectively solving the constrained problem. This is controlled by the `--interpolate-rewards` flag.

File: `rspo/storage.py`
```python
def compute_interpolate_masks(self, thresholds: torch.Tensor, alphas, use_filters, use_rnd, rnd_alpha, full_intrinsic=False):
    # ...
    likelihoods = self.returns[:-1, :, 1 + self.rnd + self.num_refs * 2:]
    failed_mask = torch.gt(thresholds, likelihoods)
    if use_filters[0]:
        extrinsic_mask = 1. - (torch.any(failed_mask, dim=2, keepdim=True)).float()
    # ...
    if use_filters[2]:
        exploration_masks = failed_mask.float()
    # ...
    masks = [alphas[0] * extrinsic_mask, ..., alphas[2] * exploration_masks]
    interpolate_masks = torch.cat(masks, dim=2)
    self.interpolate_masks.copy_(interpolate_masks)
```
The final PPO loss in `rspo/algo/ppo.py` is then computed using advantages derived from these shaped rewards.

File: `rspo/algo/ppo.py` (within `PPO.update`)
```python
# Advantages are calculated from the shaped rewards in the rollout buffer
advantages = (advantages - advantages.mean(dim=[0, 1], keepdims=True)) / (
    advantages.std(dim=[0, 1], keepdims=True) + 1e-5)
# ...
if self.args.interpolate_rewards:
    _action_loss_all = torch.mul(action_loss_all, interpolate_mask).sum(dim=-1)
    # ...
    action_loss_all = _action_loss_all * (action_loss_all.size()[0] / weight)
# ...
loss = value_loss * self.value_loss_coef + rollouts.action_loss_coef * action_loss_mask * \
       action_loss - dist_entropy * self.entropy_coef
```
The Wasserstein distance variant (SIPO-WD) is implemented via the `--dipg` flag, which computes MMD between trajectories as a proxy and adds it to the PPO loss.

File: `rspo/algo/ppo.py`
```python
if dipg:
    dipg_loss_all = action_log_probs * mmds_batch
# ...
dipg_loss = dipg_loss_all.mean()
# ...
if dipg:
    loss += dipg_loss * self.args.dipg_alpha
```
llm_extracted_info.experimental_info:
The method is built upon the PPO algorithm (`--algo ppo`). The iterative learning framework is achieved by running experiments sequentially, where policies from previous runs are loaded as references using `--ref-config`, `--ref-load-dir`, and `--ref-load-step`. The core SIPO mechanism is enabled by `--use-reference`.

The state-distance diversity measure, corresponding to the SIPO-RBF implementation, is based on the negative log-likelihood of the current policy's actions under reference policies. The weight of this intrinsic reward (Lagrange multiplier) is controlled by `--likelihood-alpha`. The optimization problem is solved by shaping rewards based on a `--likelihood-threshold`, which is enabled with `--interpolate-rewards`. This threshold determines whether to use the extrinsic task reward or the intrinsic diversity reward.

The SIPO-WD variant is enabled using the `--dipg` flag. It calculates the MMD between trajectories of the current and reference policies as a proxy for Wasserstein distance. This MMD term is added to the PPO objective, weighted by `--dipg-alpha`.

Key hyperparameters from `rspo/arguments.py` include:
- `lr`: learning rate (default: 7e-4)
- `gamma`: discount factor (default: 0.99)
- `ppo_epoch`: number of PPO epochs (default: 4)
- `num_mini_batch`: number of mini-batches for PPO (default: 32)
- `entropy_coef`: entropy coefficient (default: 0.01)
- `value_loss_coef`: value loss coefficient (default: 0.5)
- `hidden_size`: hidden layer size for the policy network (default: 64)
- `num_env_steps`: total environment steps for training (default: 10e6)

new_method.method:
{"Motivation":"Current autoguidance methods are fundamentally limited by their rigid assumptions about a problem's solution space. Quality Diversity (QD) approaches act as 'map-fillers,' efficiently populating a behavioral map whose dimensions and structure are predefined by a human expert. Conversely, hierarchical methods like HOLMES are 'map-fracturers,' dynamically building a tree by irreversibly splitting behavioral niches, which leads to permanent fragmentation and prevents the system from correcting or abstracting its understanding. This research introduces Dynamic Behavioral Cartography (DBC), a novel paradigm that treats discovery not as filling or fracturing a map, but as the continuous learning and refinement of the map itself. DBC introduces a crucial, missing operation—niche unification (merging)—enabling the system to revise its own structural hypotheses, correct early mischaracterizations, and form higher-level abstractions. It thus learns an emergent, data-driven ontology of the problem space, directly addressing the foundational rigidity of both static QD maps and append-only hierarchical structures.","Methodology":"DBC is implemented as a novel Repertoire and Emitter pair, designed to directly replace the static grid and variation operators in modern QD frameworks like PBT-MAP-ELITES.
1. **Dynamic Graph Repertoire**: The static repertoire is replaced with a dynamic graph where nodes represent behavioral 'Niches.' Each Niche contains a population of elite solutions and trains a local Variational Autoencoder (VAE) to model its specific behavioral region. A 'Global Coordinator' VAE is trained on samples across all Niches, creating a unified latent space. The graph's weighted edges are dynamically computed based on the Wasserstein-1 distance between the latent distributions of Niches in this global space, capturing the learned topology of the solution landscape.
2. **Principled Niche Specialization (Splitting)**: A Niche splits only when a principled statistical test (e.g., Hartigan's Dip Test applied to the principal components of its local VAE's latent space) detects significant multi-modality. This provides a data-driven trigger indicating that the Niche has discovered distinct sub-behaviors that require specialized representation. This is a direct improvement over HOLMES's heuristic of splitting on loss plateaus.
3. **Adaptive Niche Unification (Merging)**: To overcome the permanent fragmentation of HOLMES, DBC introduces a merge operation. Two connected Niches are unified if their latent distributions become statistically indistinguishable in the Global Coordinator's space (i.e., their Wasserstein distance falls below a dynamic threshold). This self-correction mechanism is the core innovation, allowing the system to form higher-level abstractions by merging previously distinct behaviors (e.g., 'walking' and 'hopping') into a more general 'locomotion' category as its understanding matures.
4. **Topology-Aware Latent Emitter**: The emitter leverages the learned graph structure for intelligent exploration. It is a hybrid strategy that generates new candidates by:
    a) **Exploitation:** Improving elites via mutation within their home Niche.
    b) **Bridging:** Performing latent space interpolation between elites from highly-connected adjacent Niches using the Global Coordinator's decoder. This explicitly searches the learned boundaries between known behaviors, a more principled approach than simple parameter-space crossover.
    c) **Frontier Discovery:** Sampling from an archive of solutions that are poorly reconstructed by all local VAEs but well-reconstructed by the Global Coordinator, identifying them as coherent but fundamentally novel behaviors that can seed new Niches.","Expected impact":"DBC represents a paradigm shift from solution archival to structural discovery in autoguidance. By introducing the ability to merge and unify behavioral niches, it overcomes the critical limitation of irreversible fragmentation found in HOLMES. By learning the map's structure dynamically, it transcends the static, human-defined nature of traditional QD. The primary outcome is that the resulting dynamic graph is not merely a container for diverse solutions but an emergent and interpretable scientific discovery in its own right—a learned taxonomy of the problem domain. For instance, in robotics, DBC could initially split gaits into 'walking' and 'crawling,' use its Bridging emitter to discover a transitional 'trotting' gait along the boundary, and ultimately merge all three into a unified 'locomotion' super-niche. This capacity for structural self-correction and abstraction makes DBC a powerful engine for generating novel hypotheses and insights in complex domains like robotics, drug discovery, and materials science."}

{"title": "Verifying Emergent Behavioral Abstraction in Robotics with Dynamic Behavioral Cartography (DBC)", "hypothesis": "The DBC algorithm, through its novel mechanisms of data-driven niche splitting and unification, will autonomously construct a meaningful, hierarchical representation of a robot's behavior space. We hypothesize that DBC will first discover distinct locomotion gaits (e.g., crawling, hopping) as separate niches, and subsequently merge them into a higher-level 'locomotion' super-niche as its understanding of the problem's topology matures. This emergent abstraction capability will be absent in baseline methods like the static MAP-Elites and the fragmentation-only HOLMES.", "experimental_domain": {"environment": "The experiment will be conducted using the 'ant' quadruped robot morphology within the Brax physics simulator, which provides fast, parallelized simulations suitable for large-scale evolutionary experiments.", "task": "The objective is open-ended locomotion. The robot's controller must learn to move the ant's center of mass as far as possible along the positive x-axis within a fixed simulation time (e.g., 5 seconds).", "controller_architecture": "The robot's controller will be a Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) with two hidden layers of 256 neurons each and Tanh activation functions. The input to the policy network will be the robot's proprioceptive state (joint angles, velocities, torso orientation), and the output will be the target torque for each of the 8 joints.", "fitness_function": "The fitness score is defined as the final x-coordinate of the robot's torso at the end of the simulation episode. This rewards forward locomotion.", "behavior_descriptor": "To capture the full dynamics of the learned gaits, the behavior descriptor (BD) will be a high-dimensional vector representing the robot's trajectory. Specifically, it will be the flattened sequence of the (x, y, z) coordinates of the ant's four feet, sampled at 10 Hz over the last 2 seconds of the episode. This results in a BD of size 4 feet * 3 coords * 20 samples = 240 dimensions."}, "methodology_details": {"framework": "The experiment will be implemented in PyTorch. The DBC algorithm will be integrated into a MAP-Elites-like asynchronous loop.", "dynamic_graph_repertoire": {"description": "The core of DBC. A graph where each node is a 'Niche' and edges represent behavioral similarity.", "niche_structure": "Each Niche contains a population of up to 10 elite solutions, a local VAE (Variational Autoencoder) with an MLP encoder/decoder and a 16-dimensional latent space. The VAE is trained on the behavior descriptors of the Niche's population.", "global_coordinator": "A single, larger VAE with a 32-dimensional latent space, trained on a buffer of elite samples from all active Niches. This VAE provides the unified latent space for calculating inter-niche distances.", "edge_computation": "Edges between niches are weighted by the Wasserstein-1 distance between their latent distributions, as encoded by the Global Coordinator. The graph is updated every 10 generations."}, "principled_niche_specialization": {"trigger": "A Niche is a candidate for splitting if its local VAE's reconstruction loss has plateaued and it contains a sufficient number of elites (>5).", "mechanism": "The split is executed only if a statistical test confirms multi-modality. We apply Hartigan's Dip Test for unimodality to the first principal component of the Niche's latent representations (from its local VAE). A split occurs if the test's p-value is below a significance threshold of 0.05.", "post_split": "If a split occurs, the parent Niche's solutions are partitioned into two new child Niches using K-Means clustering (k=2) in the latent space.", "code_example": "
# PyTorch-like pseudo-code for the split decision
import torch
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from diptest import dip_test # Assuming a library for Hartigan's Dip Test

def should_split_niche(niche):
    # niche.elites_bd is a tensor of behavior descriptors for the niche's elites
    if len(niche.elites_bd) < 5:
        return False
        
    # 1. Encode elites into the local latent space
    with torch.no_grad():
        latent_representations = niche.local_vae.encoder(niche.elites_bd)[0] # Get mu
    
    # 2. Apply PCA to get the principal component
    pca = PCA(n_components=1)
    principal_component = pca.fit_transform(latent_representations.cpu().numpy())
    
    # 3. Apply Hartigan's Dip Test
    dip_statistic, p_value = dip_test(principal_component)
    
    # 4. Make decision based on p-value
    return p_value < 0.05
"}, "adaptive_niche_unification": {"trigger": "Periodically (e.g., every 20 generations), we evaluate all pairs of connected Niches in the graph for potential merging.", "mechanism": "Two Niches, A and B, are merged if the statistical distance between their latent distributions becomes indistinguishable. We calculate the Wasserstein-1 distance between the two sets of latent representations (encoded by the *Global Coordinator* VAE). They are merged if this distance falls below a dynamic threshold, defined as the 25th percentile of all edge weights in the graph.", "post_merge": "If merged, the two Niches' populations are combined into a new, single Niche. The local VAE for this new Niche is re-initialized and trained from scratch on the combined population.", "code_example": "
# PyTorch-like pseudo-code for the merge decision using geomloss
import torch
import geomloss

def should_merge_niches(niche_a, niche_b, global_vae, distance_threshold):
    # 1. Encode elites from both niches using the Global Coordinator VAE
    with torch.no_grad():
        latents_a = global_vae.encoder(niche_a.elites_bd)[0]
        latents_b = global_vae.encoder(niche_b.elites_bd)[0]

    # 2. Define a Sinkhorn divergence (Wasserstein distance) object
    # p=1 for Wasserstein-1, blur=0.05 is a typical hyperparameter
    sinkhorn_loss = geomloss.SamplesLoss(loss="sinkhorn", p=1, blur=0.05)

    # 3. Calculate the distance
    distance = sinkhorn_loss(latents_a, latents_b).item()

    # 4. Make decision based on the dynamic threshold
    return distance < distance_threshold
"}, "topology_aware_latent_emitter": {"strategy_mix": "The emitter uses a 40/40/20 mix of exploitation, bridging, and frontier discovery.", "exploitation": "Selects a random elite from a random Niche and applies small Gaussian noise to its controller parameters.", "bridging": "Selects two parent elites from adjacent Niches in the graph (connected by an edge with a low Wasserstein distance). New controller parameters are generated by linear interpolation: `c_new = alpha * c_1 + (1-alpha) * c_2`, where `alpha` is sampled from a Beta distribution.", "frontier_discovery": "Maintains a small archive of solutions that are poorly reconstructed by all existing local VAEs but well-reconstructed by the Global Coordinator. experimental_analysis.analysis_report:
The experiment was designed to evaluate the efficacy of the proposed Dynamic Behavioral Cartography (DBC) method, specifically its core innovations of dynamic niche splitting and unification for creating a learned behavioral taxonomy. The experimental setup, designated 'DBC_ROBOT_ABSTRACTION', aimed to test DBC's capacity for structural self-correction and abstraction in a robotics domain, which aligns directly with the method's expected impact of discovering an emergent ontology of the problem space.

The stated goal of DBC is to overcome the fundamental limitations of static Quality Diversity (QD) maps and the irreversible fragmentation of hierarchical methods like HOLMES. Success in this experiment would be demonstrated by observing the dynamic graph of behavioral niches evolve in a specific manner: 1) Initial niches would form and specialize via the principled splitting mechanism (Hartigan's Dip Test), creating distinct categories for different robotic gaits. 2) The Topology-Aware Emitter, particularly its 'Bridging' function, would discover transitional behaviors between these niches. 3) Critically, the novel 'Unification' mechanism would activate, merging previously separate niches (e.g., 'walking' and 'hopping') into a single, higher-level abstract niche (e.g., 'locomotion') once their latent VAE distributions became statistically similar.

However, the 'Experimental results' section provided for this analysis is empty. Without any empirical data—such as logs of the graph evolution, performance metrics (e.g., QD-score, coverage), or analysis of the final discovered niches and their abstractions—it is impossible to conduct a detailed summary of the outcomes. Consequently, a conclusive assessment of whether the Dynamic Behavioral Cartography method demonstrates a clear advantage cannot be made. The methodology presents a compelling theoretical framework for advancing autoguidance, but its practical utility remains unverified pending the provision of experimental results.
